segments.s510-g41: بر is the locative preposition 'on/upon' placed before the plural noun ایوانها ('the iwans/porches'). چهر افراسیاب is a noun phrase with the head چهر ('face/visage') followed by the name افراسیاب: in classical/poetic Persian the possessed/identifier often follows the head without an overt ezāfe, so this reads 'the face of Afrasiab.'
segments.s510-g42: نگاریده is a past participle used adjectivally/predicatively ('painted, depicted'). روشن‌تر is the comparative form of روشن ('brighter/more luminous'), and از introduces the standard of comparison ('than'). The clause lacks an explicit copula (typical in classical Persian), so the participle plus comparative yields 'painted (and) brighter than the sun.'
segments.s510-g43: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' modifying چهر ('visage/face'). کیخسرو follows as the identifying proper name (apposition). جنگ‌جوی is an epithet/attribute ('warrior') linked to the name; the phrase is a compact appositive: 'that same visage — Kay Khosrow, the warrior.'
segments.s510-g44: A coordinated list of abstract nouns (بزرگی, مردی, فرهنگ) describing attributes, joined by و. The final possessive pronoun اوی attaches to the whole series and functions like 'his,' so the structure reads 'his greatness, (his) manliness, and (his) culture.' Note that مردی here means 'manliness' or 'valor' rather than simply 'a man.'
segments.s510-g45: بران is a fused locative pronoun/particle ('thereon/on those') indicating location. استخوان ('bones') is the noun, followed by the preposition بر plus نگاریده (past participle 'painted'), with پاک qualifying the painting ('clean/pure' or 'cleanly painted'). The participle modifies the preceding noun phrase: 'bones painted cleanly thereon.'
segments.s510-g46: نبینی is negative present/imperative-like third-person form 'one does not / you will not see.' به شهر اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/into the inner part of the city' (به ... اندرون = 'in the interior of the city'), and گرد و خاک is a coordinated noun phrase 'dust and dirt.' Together: 'you will not see dust and dirt in the city's inner quarters.'
segments.s510-g47: This is a slightly inverted classical word order. ز ماهی بود introduces the source/material ('from fish was...'), with بود the past copula 'was.' مردمان را خورش is a definite phrase 'the people's food' where را marks the definite/affected noun; overall: 'Fish served as the people's food.'
segments.s510-g48: ندارند is third-person plural present 'they do not have/possess' (here negative). چیزی جزین is an indefinite-exceptive phrase 'anything except this' (جز + demonstrative), and پرورش is the noun 'upbringing/nourishment/development' — here 'nourishment' or 'raising' in a figurative sense. The clause says they possess nothing except this kind of sustenance/education.
segments.s510-g17: سوی ژرف دریا is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward the deep sea' placed before the verb. همی is an archaic/progressive adverb (continuous or iterative) that precedes the past plural verb راندند ('they drove/sailed'); راندند is 3rd-person plural past, here meaning 'they drove' (ships/people) toward that direction.
segments.s510-g18: جهان‌آفرین را places 'the Creator' as a definite/affected object with the object marker را. همی خواندند combines the same progressive/iterative همی with the past plural خواندند ('they called'); خواندند here means 'to call/summon' (they called out to the Creator). Word order (object before verb) is normal for Persian with را-marked objects.
segments.s510-g19: This is a stacked noun phrase: دد و دام بد = 'enemies and evil traps' (nouns coordinated by و), with بد functioning as an adjective applying to the coordinated nouns. هر سوی بی‌شمار ('on every side, countless') is a locational/quantifying phrase modifying the situation. Word order places modifiers after the nouns in an older/poetic style.
segments.s510-g20: سپه را uses را to mark the affected entity ('for the host/army'). نبد is an archaic negative copula/past-form meaning 'was not' or 'did not exist' (3rd-person). خوردنی functions as a noun/adjective 'edible/food', and جز شکار 'except (for) prey' gives the sole exception. The clause thus says there was no food for the army except prey.
segments.s510-g21: پدید آمد is a compound verbal idea 'appeared/came into view' (پدید + آمد). از دور is a locative phrase 'from afar'. مردی is indefinite 'a man' and سترگ is an epithet modifying him; the adjective follows the noun in this poetic word order. (Note: سترگ in the provided sense list does not match the expected meaning here, so its precise lexical sense is irregular in the list.)
segments.s510-g22: پر از موی is an adjectival phrase 'full of hair' where پر takes از + noun to mean 'filled with X'. با گوشهای بزرگ is a separate prepositional phrase 'with large ears' introduced by با. Together they further describe the man introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s510-g23: تنش is 'his body' (تن + possessive enclitic -ش). زیر موی اندرون literally 'under the hair, on the inside' is a locative phrase modifying the body. همچو introduces a simile 'like/as', and نیل here is used as a color simile ('indigo/dark blue'), so the clause says the skin under his hair was indigo-colored.
segments.s510-g24: دو گوشش 'his two ears' (numeral + noun + enclitic) is followed by به کردار 'in the likeness/form of' (an idiomatic classical expression). دو گوش پیل 'the two ears of an elephant' is the comparison; پیل 'elephant' is used figuratively to indicate very large ears. کردار here functions as 'likeness/shape' in an archaic poetic sense.
segments.s510-g1: This is a simple past-clause with subject + object + prepositional goal + verb. "سکندر" is the subject, "سپه را" is the definite object marked by the object marker را (showing the direct object), "به بابل" is the prepositional phrase indicating destination, and "کشید" is the past tense verb (3rd‑person singular) meaning 'drew/led/moved' in this context.
segments.s510-g2: Poetic inversion: the agental/directional phrase "ز گرد سپه" ('from the dust/whirl of the host') comes first for emphasis, followed by the verb/copula "شد" ('became') and the subject/predicate "هوا ناپدید" ('the air [became] vanished'). In normal prose you would expect "هوا ناپدید شد."
segments.s510-g3: "همی" is an archaic/poetic adverb indicating continuous or prolonged action (roughly 'kept on / continuously'). "راند" is the past-tense verb 'drove/rode' (3rd person). "یک ماه" expresses duration ('for one month'). "خود" here is a reflexive/emphatic 'himself' (emphatic), and "با سپاه" is an instrumental/companional phrase 'with the army.' Word order places the duration after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s510-g4: Negative past-tense clause: "ندیدند" is the past 3rd‑person plural negative 'they did not see.' "زیشان" ('ز ایشان') is a prepositional/genitive 'of/from them' indicating the group concerned. "کس" in a negated clause functions as 'anyone/any' → effectively 'no one/none' here, followed by the noun "آرامگاه" ('resting place/tomb') as the thing not seen. The compact word order omits extra particles common in prose but is normal in epic poetry.
segments.s510-g5: "بدین‌گونه" is an adverb meaning 'in this manner/thus.' "تا" here means 'as far as' (marking extent), "سوی" is the directional preposition 'toward(s),' and "کوهی" with the adjectival/indefinite suffix -ی functions as 'a certain mountain' / 'a mountain.' "رسید" is the past 3rd‑person singular 'arrived/reached.'
segments.s510-g6: Poetic compact clause: "ز" = 'from,' then "دیدار" here introduces the notion of 'sight/seeing' (used elliptically), "دیده" is the noun 'eye,' so the phrase as a whole means roughly 'from the sight of the eye.' "سرش" = "سر + ش" ('his head') with the possessive clitic, and "ناپدید" is the predicate adjective 'vanished/invisible.' Word order places the possessive clitic on the noun as usual (سرش = 'his head').
segments.s510-g7: "به سر بر" is a stacked prepositional phrase meaning 'on the head/upon the summit' (به = 'to/at', سر = 'top/head', بر = 'on/upon'). "یکی" is the indefinite article-like 'a certain' or 'one,' "ابر تاریک" is the noun phrase 'dark cloud,' and "بود" is the past tense copula 'was.' The clause describes location with the locative prepositional sequence preceding the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s510-g8: "به کیوان تو گفتی" is an indirect speech construction: "به کیوان" = 'to Kayvān' (dative), "تو" is the 2nd‑person singular subject 'you,' and "گفتی" is the past-tense verb 'you said' (here introducing reported speech). The conjunction "که" introduces the reported content, and "نزدیک بود" is the subordinate clause 'it was near' with "نزدیک" as predicate adjective and "بود" the past copula.
segments.s510-g57: 'همه' precedes the possessed noun to mean 'all' (all of them). 'جامه‌هاشان' is the plural noun 'garments/robes' with the possessive suffix -شان ('their'). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'of/from' marking material (here 'made of'), followed by the materials 'خز و حریر' (velvet and silk). Word order is literal: 'all their garments of velvet and silk.'
segments.s510-g58: 'ازو' here means 'of them' or 'among them' and refers back to the people just mentioned. 'چند' is used colloquially as 'some' (not asking quantity). 'برنا' = 'young' (a young person). 'بد' in this classical/poetic text is an archaic short form of 'بود' (was) and functions as the past copula 'were' (not the usual adjective 'bad'); I mark it as archaic. The second 'چند' again = 'some', and 'پیر' = 'old (person)'. The whole clause says 'of them some were young and some old.'
segments.s510-g59: 'ازو' = 'among them/of them'. 'هرک' is an archaic/poetic equivalent of 'هر که' meaning 'whoever' or 'each one who'. 'پیری' here denotes being old (an elder). 'بد' again is the archaic past-copula 'بود' = 'was'. 'و نام داشت' literally 'and had a name' = 'and was named/known' (i.e., had a reputation or name). Together: 'Among them, whoever was an elder and had a name...'
segments.s510-g60: 'پر از' is a fixed construction meaning 'full of'. The following word (here written 'در') in context is a poetic/archaic word for jewels/precious stones (not the ordinary 'door'); because that sense is not given in the lemma list I mark it as -1. 'زرین' = 'golden' modifies the jewels. 'یکی جام داشت' = 'he had one cup' or better in natural English 'had a cup' — note the order: the indefinite numeral 'یکی' precedes the noun 'جام', and 'داشت' is the past-tense verb 'had'. The whole clause: 'he had a cup full of golden jewels.'
segments.s510-g61: 'کسی' = 'someone'. 'کو' here is an archaic relative/connector (classical usage parallel to 'که') introducing a relative clause: 'someone who...'. 'جوان بود' = 'was young' (بود is past of 'to be'). 'تاجی به دست' = 'with a crown in (his) hand'; 'تاجی' is adjectival/relational to 'tāj' (pertaining to a crown) and the phrase 'به دست' expresses 'in hand' or 'holding'. The whole phrase describes a person: 'someone who was young, (with) a crown in hand.'
segments.s510-g62: 'بر' + person (قیصر) here means motion/toward or 'before' someone (i.e., 'came before the Caesar'). 'آمد' is simple past 'came/approached'. 'سرافگنده پست' are two adjectival predicates describing his demeanour: 'سرافگنده' = ashamed/embarrassed and 'پست' = lowly/humble; they follow the verb and jointly qualify the subject's appearance on approaching the Caesar.
segments.s510-g63: 'برفتند' is the past plural 'they went/left'. 'و' = 'and'. 'بردند' is past plural 'they carried/brought'; here it goes with the object 'نماز' (prayer/ritual). 'پیشش' = 'before him / in his presence'. The clause literally: 'They went and brought (it) before him, the prayer.'
segments.s510-g64: 'بگفتند' is past plural 'they spoke/said'. 'با او' = 'with him'. 'زمانی دراز' is an adverbial phrase 'for a long time' ('زمانی' used adverbially = 'a time/for a while', modified by 'دراز' = 'long'). Together: 'they spoke with him for a long time.'
segments.s510-g9: This clause literally orders as prepositional phrase + adverb + verb + object. به جایی (به + جایی) means “toward/to any place”; بروبر is an adverb meaning “straight ahead / directly”; ندیدند is past‑tense plural negative “they did not see”; راه is the object “path/way.” Together: “they did not see a path straight to any place.”
segments.s510-g10: فروماند is a past/inchoative sense meaning ‘was left/failed/strayed’ (here passive/intransitive sense of being left behind or cut off). از is the preposition “from,” and راه شاه و سپاه is the genitive phrase “the road/way of the king and the host.” The whole segment says that (someone/it) was left behind or separated from the king’s and army’s road.
segments.s510-g11: گذشتند is the past plural ‘they passed.’ بر کوه خارا is a prepositional phrase ‘over/on the flinty/rocky mountain’ (بر + noun + adjective), and به رنج is an adverbial phrase ‘with hardship’ where به functions as an adverbial marker (‘by/with’).
segments.s510-g12: This clause has an introductory/locative particle (وزو) that is difficult/archaic in this context (see note), then خیره شد ‘became astonished’ (خیره as predicate/adjective + شد as past auxiliary). مرد باریک سنج is an epithet: مرد ‘man’ followed by a compound modifier باریک سنج (‘barik‑sanj’ = finely‑measuring / subtle‑judging). The structure is: at that (thing) the finely‑judging man was astonished.
segments.s510-g13: ز is the archaic/poetic preposition ‘from,’ رفتن is the infinitive ‘going,’ and چو introduces the temporal clause ‘when.’ گشتند is past plural ‘they became/turned,’ یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely/wholly,’ and ستوه is the adjective ‘weary.’ So: ‘when they became entirely weary from going…’
segments.s510-g14: یکی here means ‘one/someone’ (one of them). ژرف دریا is the noun phrase ‘the deep sea.’ بد is problematic/archaic in this position (its ordinary senses don’t neatly fit the likely meaning here), followed by آن روی کوه ‘that side/face of the mountain’ (آن = ‘that’, روی = ‘side/surface’, کوه = ‘mountain’). In context the clause is elliptical: ‘one (of them) [saw] the deep sea on that side of the mountain,’ with the verb omitted.
segments.s510-g15: پدید آمد is a past verb phrase ‘appeared / came into view.’ و شاد شد is a coordinate clause ‘and became glad.’ زان here means ‘that (as a result/of that)’ or ‘therefore/that (army)’, and سپاه names the subject ‘the army/host.’ Word order is slightly inverted (verb before subject) as common in older poetic Persian.
segments.s510-g16: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ‘for/that,’ دریا و هامون is a coordinated direct object ‘the sea and the plain,’ بدیدند is past plural ‘they saw,’ and راه (here) names the route/way connected to what was seen. The clause gives the reason: they rejoiced because they saw the sea and the plain and the way.
segments.s510-g33: بپرسید is a narrative past/formulaic speech form meaning 'they asked' (classical epic usage). کان چیست is an archaic demonstrative + interrogative: کان 'that' + چی 'what' + ست (poetic form of است) 'is', giving 'what is that'. بمیان آب is a prepositional phrase (بـ- + میان) 'in the midst (of) the water'.
segments.s510-g34: کزان is a relative/comparative particle 'from which' or 'whence'; سوی is directional 'toward(s)'; می‌برزند is a present/imperfect third-person plural verb with the prefix می- meaning 'appear'/'rise forth' (poetic/habitual present), and آفتاب is the noun 'the sun' (here the thing that appears). Together: 'from which the sun appears.' Note the plural verb is a common epic usage referring to the phenomenon (rays) or used generically.
segments.s510-g35: ازان پس چنین گفت is a narrative phrase: ازان پس 'after that', چنین 'thus', گفت is the past-tense verb 'he said' (classical narration). کای شهریار is a vocative: کای 'O' + شهریار 'sovereign/king' addressing the ruler.
segments.s510-g36: همیشه is an adverb 'always/ever'; بدی here functions as a noun 'goodness/good deed(s)' (contextual meaning — the provided senses list lacks this sense); در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world'; نامدار is a predicate adjective 'renowned/illustrious', so the phrase praises the king as 'ever renowned in the world for (his) goodness.'
segments.s510-g37: The line opens with an inverted predication: یکی 'a certain' (indefinite) + شارستانست (شارستان + -ست, fused copula) 'is a city' followed by این 'this' and چون 'like/as' + بهشت 'paradise' — i.e. 'This is a city, one like paradise.'
segments.s510-g38: که introduces the subordinate clause; گویی here functions idiomatically as 'it seems / as if' (a parenthetical comment), از indicates origin 'from', خاک 'the earth/soil', دارد is the present/past auxiliary 'has/possesses' and سرشت 'nature/constitution' — together: 'as if its nature were not of the earth' (the negative نه appears in the original clause but is not listed among the segment's word entries).
segments.s510-g39: نبینی is a negative present/generalizing form 'you do not / would not see' used as a general statement; بدواندر (بـ + در + اندر) means 'therein/inside'; ایوان و خان lists architectural spaces 'the iwan and the khān/hall' (خان here names a building/large hall). The phrase means 'you will not see (inside) the iwan and khan...'.
segments.s510-g40: مگر introduces an exception 'except/except for'; پوشش is 'a covering/ornament/veneer'; از marks the material 'of/from'; ماهی here denotes 'fish' (used as material); و 'and'; استخوان 'bone'. So: 'except coverings of fish and bone.'
segments.s510-g65: ببود is an archaic past copula meaning 'it was' placed before the time expression (poetic word order). آن شب = 'that night' (temporal subject/setting). و گاه = 'and at the hour/at that time' (و simply links the temporal phrase). بانگِ خروس is a genitive phrase 'the cry of the rooster' (بانگ = 'cry/call', خروس = 'rooster') that specifies the sound heard.
segments.s510-g66: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; درگاه here means the palace/gate/threshold ('from the gate/court'). برخاست is a past intransitive verb 'rose/was lifted' (the action). آوای کوس = 'the sound/voice of the drum' (آوا + ezāfe + کوس) is the noun phrase that acts as the thing which 'rose' (i.e., 'the drum's sound rose'). Word order is elevated/poetic: prepositional phrase + verb + nominal phrase.
segments.s510-g67: وزان = وز + از (here fused) meaning 'and from that' or 'thereupon/from that place', linking to the previous action. جایگه = 'place/spot' and combines with وزان to mean 'from that spot'. سوی بابل = a directional phrase 'toward Babylon'. کشید is the past of کشیدن used in poetic narrative to mean 'moved/advanced/marched' (here intransitive: 'he moved toward'). Note: the printed sense list did not include this motion meaning, so the sense index for کشید is not applicable.
segments.s510-g68: زمین is the subject 'the earth/land'; گشت (from گشتن) here means 'became/turned' (copular/inchoative use). از لشکرش = 'because of/from his army' (the prepositional phrase indicates cause or that the army covered the land). ناپدید is a predicative adjective 'vanished/hidden/unseen' describing زمین after گشت. Together: 'the earth became unseen/hidden because of his army.'
segments.s510-g25: چو is the archaic/poetic conjunction 'when' (not 'as/like' here). دیدند is past plural 'they saw' (root دید#بین). گردنکشان is a plural agent noun 'the rebels/proud-necked ones'. زان is the demonstrative contraction 'از + آن' meaning 'that' or 'from that' and here marks which sign is meant; نشان = 'sign' — together: 'when the rebels saw that sign...'.
segments.s510-g26: ببردند is the past tense (بردن) 'they took/carried (away)'. پیش here means 'in the presence of/before' (i.e. before Alexander). سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander'. کشان is a present-participial/adverbial form from کشاندن 'dragging' that attaches after the clause to qualify how they carried it — 'they carried (it), dragging (it)'.
segments.s510-g27: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. نگه کرد is a light-verb construction (نگه 'gaze' + کرد 'did') meaning 'he looked / fixed his gaze'. زو is the older contracted prepositional form (از + او) meaning 'at it / at him' (i.e. 'at that'). خیره ماند is a compound 'خیره' (staring/astonished) + ماند (remained) meaning 'he remained staring' or 'stood astonished.'
segments.s510-g28: بروبر is an adverb 'face-to-face / directly before (him)'. همی is the habitual/progressive particle (classical usage) meaning 'continually/kept on'. نام یزدان is the noun phrase 'the name of God' (نام = 'name', یزدان = 'God/divine'). بخواند is a third-person narrative-form verb from خواندن; with همی it expresses repeated/persistent action — 'he called/uttered/invoked'.
segments.s510-g29: چه مردی here introduces an actor — 'a (certain) man' or 'what man' used idiomatically as 'a man'. بدو = به + او 'to him'. گفت is the past 'said' (here used as the speech verb introducing direct speech). نام تو چیست is the standard word order for 'What is your name?' (نام = 'name', تو = 'you', چیست = 'what is').
segments.s510-g30: ز دریا = 'from the sea' (ز = 'from', دریا = 'sea'). چه یابی is an interrogative present/subjunctive of یابیدن 'to find/obtain' meaning 'what do you get/find?' (classical Persian often uses this form for general questions). و = 'and'. کام تو چیست = 'what is your desire?' (کام = desire/wish; تو = you; چیست = is what).
segments.s510-g31: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = he said). شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. م + را here function together as the object marker 'مرا' (me / for me) in older poetic spacing: 'مرا' = 'for me' or 'me' (object). باب و مام are dialectal/traditional forms for 'father and mother' (مام = mother). So this clause begins the reply: 'He said to him, "O king, (for) me father and mother..."' (i.e. introducing what his parents did).
segments.s510-g32: همان = 'that / the same'. گوش بستر is a compound image 'the ear (side) of the bed/cradle' (گوش = ear, بستر = bed/cradle). نهادند is the past transitive 'they placed/put' (نهادند from نهادن). نام = 'name' (object). Together: 'they placed the name in that ear of the bed' — i.e. when setting him in the bed/cradle they put the name into his ear (a poetic way to say they named him).
segments.s510-g49: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. فرمان دهد is a present/subjunctive-style clause ('gives/should give the order'), with دهد (3rd-person) as the reporting verb. نامبردار شاه is a noun phrase 'the renowned king' (epithet + noun) that serves as the subject of دهد.
segments.s510-g50: روم attaches to the preceding شاه (forming 'king of Rum'); the phrase then continues with the speaker's subject من ('I'). بران شارستان is an old-style locution = بر + آن + شارستان, 'to/onto that city', and بی‌سپاه is an adverbial/ adjectival phrase 'without an army'. The main verb ('I will go') is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s510-g51: سکندر is the subject. بدان گوش is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'on/at that hearing' (ب + دان + گوش), and ور is a classical particle here meaning 'thereupon/then'. گفت is the speech verb 'said', which introduces reported speech; رو begins the quoted imperative (literally 'turn/face' but functions as a directive to come forward).
segments.s510-g52: بیاور is the 2nd-person imperative 'bring' (command). کسی is the direct object 'someone'. تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that') followed by the subjunctive/optative بینیم ('we may see'); چه is the interrogative 'what' and نو qualifies it as 'new'.
segments.s510-g53: بشد is a past/inchoative verb 'it happened/it became' used impersonally or with an implied subject. گوش بستر ('ears of the bed') is a poetic expression meaning 'those lying in bed paid attention' (گوش = attention, بستر = bed), هم 'also', اندر زمان 'at that time'.
segments.s510-g54: ازان = 'from that' (referring back to the city). شارستان = 'city'. برد is a past verb from بردن ('took/captured/ carried off') here 'they took' or 'seized' (poetic compression). مردم = 'the people', and دمان (دمان) = 'hem(s)/skirts' (an idiom often used to express clutching garments in flight).
segments.s510-g55: گذشتند is the simple past (3rd-person plural) 'they passed/crossed'. بر آب = 'over/on the water' (direction or surface), and هفتاد مرد = 'seventy men' (numeral + noun). The verb governs the following locative phrase.
segments.s510-g56: خرد یافته is a compound participial phrase 'having acquired wisdom' (خرد 'wisdom' + یافته 'found/acquired') modifying مردم 'people'. سالخورد is an adjective 'aged/old' giving the sense 'wise, experienced, and elderly people.'

sentences.s510-s21: On the iwans was the visage of Afrasiab, painted brighter than the sun.
sentences.s510-s22: That same visage — Kay Khosrow, the warrior — his greatness, manliness, and culture.
sentences.s510-s23: On those (panels) bones were painted cleanly; you will not see dust and dirt in the inner parts of the city.
sentences.s510-s24: From fish was the people's food; they have nothing except this nourishment/upbringing.
sentences.s510-s9: They drove toward the deep sea, and they called upon the Creator.
sentences.s510-s10: On every side were countless enemies and evil traps; for the host there was no food except prey.
sentences.s510-s11: From afar there appeared a mighty man, covered with hair and with large ears.
sentences.s510-s12: Beneath his hair his body was like indigo; his two ears were like the two ears of an elephant.
sentences.s510-s1: Alexander led the army to Babylon; from the host's dust the air vanished.
sentences.s510-s2: He rode on with his army for a month; they found no resting place among them.
sentences.s510-s3: Thus he came up to a mountain; from sight his head disappeared.
sentences.s510-s4: On its summit there was a dark cloud; you told Kayvān that it was near.
sentences.s510-s29: All their robes were of velvet and silk; among them some were young and some were old.
sentences.s510-s30: Among them, whoever was an elder and had a name had a cup full of golden jewels.
sentences.s510-s31: A young man, with a crown in his hand, came before the Caesar, ashamed and humble.
sentences.s510-s32: They went and brought (it) before him — they spoke with him for a long time.
sentences.s510-s5: They saw no direct way anywhere; he was left behind from the road of the king and his host.
sentences.s510-s6: They passed over the flinty mountain with hardship, and at that sight the finely judging man was astonished.
sentences.s510-s7: When, from the going, they became wholly weary, one (of them) saw the deep sea on that side of the mountain.
sentences.s510-s8: It appeared, and that host rejoiced, for they had seen the sea and the plain and the way.
sentences.s510-s17: They asked, "What is that in the middle of the water, from which the sun appears?"
sentences.s510-s18: After that he said thus, 'O king, ever renowned in the world for goodness.'
sentences.s510-s19: This city is one like paradise; it seems as if its nature were not of the earth.
sentences.s510-s20: In the iwans and halls you would see nothing but coverings made of fish and bone.
sentences.s510-s33: It was that night, and at that hour the rooster crowed from the gate and the sound of the drum arose.
sentences.s510-s34: From that place he marched toward Babylon; the earth was hidden by his army.
sentences.s510-s13: When the rebels saw that sign, they carried it before Alexander, dragging (it).
sentences.s510-s14: Alexander fixed his gaze and stood astonished; right in front of him he repeatedly invoked the name of God.
sentences.s510-s15: A man said to him, 'What is your name? From the sea what do you get, and what is your desire?'.
sentences.s510-s16: He said to him, 'O king, for me my father and mother — when they laid me on the bed they placed the name in that ear.'
sentences.s510-s25: When the renowned king of Rum gives the order, I will go to that city without an army.
sentences.s510-s26: Alexander, on hearing that, then said: 'Bring someone here so that we may see what is new.'
sentences.s510-s27: At that moment even those in their beds pricked up their ears; from that city the people seized their hems and fled (lit. 'took the hems').
sentences.s510-s28: Seventy men crossed over the water; they were wise and aged people.
